description:
You are an automation-workflow specialist who wants to standardise the way new Python helpers are deployed.  
Please prepare a minimal, fully reproducible pip environment and leave behind two artefacts that continuous-integration jobs can later inspect.

1. Make sure the directory  
   /home/user/workflows  
   exists before you do anything else.

2. Inside your home directory, create a virtual environment named  
   /home/user/automation_env  
   (use the standard venv module – **do not** rely on virtualenv, poetry, conda, etc.).

3. Activate that virtual environment and install **exactly** the versions below (no extras, no different versions):  
   • requests==2.31.0  
   • schedule==1.2.1  

4. After the installation succeeds, produce the two files shown below **with precisely the contents and ordering given**.  
   The automated grader will read these files byte-for-byte, so make sure to respect capitalisation, spacing, new-line placement and final line-feeds.

   a) /home/user/workflows/pip_install.log  
      – must contain **three** lines and three lines only, in this exact order:  
        REQUESTS_STATUS=INSTALLED  
        SCHEDULE_STATUS=INSTALLED  
        VENV_PATH=/home/user/automation_env  

        (Each line must end with a Unix line-feed “\n”; there must be **no** blank line at the end.)

   b) /home/user/workflows/requirements.lock  
      – must contain exactly two lines:  
        requests==2.31.0  
        schedule==1.2.1  

        (Again, each line ends with a single Unix line-feed and there is no trailing blank line.)

Hints & boundaries (not visible to the grader, but helpful for you):
• Only 3–4 shell commands are necessary (mkdir, python3 -m venv, pip install, echo/cat redirect).  
• You do NOT need root privileges; keep everything under /home/user.  
• Make sure the virtual environment is created before pip install, otherwise the packages will leak into the global interpreter.  
• Do not add any additional packages, comments or metadata to the two output files.  
• Remember to activate the venv (source /home/user/automation_env/bin/activate) before calling pip.

When you are done, the grader will check that:
• /home/user/automation_env exists and looks like a valid venv.  
• The two files under /home/user/workflows exist and match the exact byte-content listed above.  
• The virtual environment’s site-packages contains requests 2.31.0 and schedule 1.2.1.

Good luck!
truth:
Expected artefacts for verification:

1. File: /home/user/workflows/pip_install.log  
   Exact 3-line content (with \n line endings):  
   REQUESTS_STATUS=INSTALLED\n  
   SCHEDULE_STATUS=INSTALLED\n  
   VENV_PATH=/home/user/automation_env\n

2. File: /home/user/workflows/requirements.lock  
   Exact 2-line content (with \n line endings):  
   requests==2.31.0\n  
   schedule==1.2.1\n

3. Directory: /home/user/automation_env  
   – sub-directories bin/, lib/, etc. present as created by python3 -m venv.  
   – Inside lib/python*/site-packages, metadata for both packages at the specified versions must be present (e.g., requests-2.31.0.dist-info, schedule-1.2.1.dist-info).  
   – Activating the venv and running “python -m pip show requests” must report Version: 2.31.0; similarly for schedule.

These elements are what the automated tests will assert against; no other checks are required.
name: task_000000_d1c6c341
